narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your undergraduate GPA of 3.70, which stands out well above the typical student's average of 3.21, and your upper-middle family income background. You're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of students at 92.7%, and your LSAT score of 29.50 reflects solid preparation efforts. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 49.6% probability that you would fail it—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of around 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school lagged behind peers. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also far under the typical 5.7 average, added to that concern as the next most influential pull toward failure. On a brighter note, your strong undergraduate GPA had the strongest counterbalancing effect, strongly pushing the prediction toward bar exam success and helping keep your failure odds below 50%. Your LSAT score provided meaningful support in the same positive direction, while your family income and full-time status offered smaller boosts toward passing. Compared to typical students, your early and mid-school deciles were in the bottom tier, which weighed heavily against you despite your standout college record. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—it's the model highlighting patterns from past bar exam data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to the 36.7 average, strengthening your school performance references if possible, or targeting programs with holistic reviews that value your unique background. You have the GPA foundation to build on—keep pushing, and many doors will open. We're rooting for you.
